Simple and sleek, this workstation highlights modern functionality in a cosy setting.

How did a home bridge cultures between the North and the South, the East and the West?

In this sprawling home by Renesa Architecture Design Studio @renesa.architects, engraved Chettinadu pillars, antique chandeliers, vernacular furniture and a constant air of culture that wafts around honouring the owners’ Kerala heritage. But you are not in Kerala but in Pune! While it’s tough to put a finger on what the pièce de résistance, one thing is certain: a fusion of culture with contemporary living.

The House of Gond, joint vision of homeowner Monica Gaur and Sanchit Arora @isanchitarora and his team, pierces together the bells and whistles of this airy 3,000 sq ft apartment, while radiating evident Kerala tharavadu energy, amplified by openness and ample natural lighting. #EDHomes

Tour the home from the link in bio 🔗

Text by Pooja Prabbhan Srijith @lilasblog_05
Photography by Avesh Gaur @avesh.gaur

Furniture: @studio.fragments
Lighting: @whitelightingsolutions
Art & decor: @mayankgoyal55
Flooring & cladding: @parmandesigns
Immensely warm, cosy and charming, the contrasting combination of the cabinetry achieves a striking yet balanced aesthetic.

“The clues were all around,” says Swati Seraan @swatiseraan of By the Riverside @bytheriverside.in⁠ about this home in Bengaluru, “In the silk sarees the woman of the house wore to work each day, in their collection of antique furniture, and in the way they carried their heritage with quiet pride.” Brick was laid along the ceiling, silk was stitched into wardrobes, and the furniture that had been passed down remained where it had always been. While reviving the home, they took the cues from what they saw.⁠
⁠
Every material used in this house has precedent in South Indian domestic architecture. Bricks are rearticulated in ceiling borders and laid in alternating bonds — stack, herringbone and free form — creating rhythm throughout the space. Brass comes in through habit, it’s on coffee filter handles, window latches, prayer lamps, and cupboard locks—things still in use, still in place. You could be sitting in the carved-out reading nook by the window, surrounded by soft pink walls and a library’s worth of books. Or lounging in the bedroom, textured like a bowl of warm payasam—simple but quietly generous. Vintage sarees were deftly refashioned into wardrobe inlays, upholstery, and layers of soft furnishings. The fabrics came from the family’s own cupboard — sarees worn to work, to weddings, and to quiet mornings at home. They were folded, cut, and stitched into panels, cushions, and coverings. Their jewel tones like indigo, peacock green, gold and rust offer a material continuity that ties rooms together like a well-worn pallu. #EDHomes⁠
⁠
Tour the home the link in bio 🔗⁠
⁠
Text by Disha Kalyankar @dishakalyankar ⁠
Photography by Nayan Soni @nayansoni21⁠

Could a Chettinad mansion prove to be the blueprint for a modern-day home? Shaped with garnet Athangudi tiles, refurbished antiques and colour aplenty, this 2,400 sq ft home in Bengaluru by Studio Anvaya @studioanvaya skillfully remodels old-world grandeur for the 21st century.⁠
⁠
With Palakkad roots and a Tamil-Brahmin upbringing, the homeowners deeply admired the craftsmanship of their childhood dwellings. This strong tug of remembrance seeped into the imaginative framework they shared with the architect. Think breezy Thinnais (porches), carved colonnades, Belgian stained glass, lime-plastered walls and brass accents. The emanating result is a Chettinad-style home reimagined with a contemporary demeanour. Colour-speckled flooring, subtly shifting levels and heirlooms aplenty define the interiors, resulting in "a crossover between what the family has painstakingly held onto and newer additions, enabling the latter to become heirlooms for the future," concludes Ashwathy CK @ashwathyck, Principal Architect at Studio Anvaya.⁠
⁠
Tour the home from the link in our bio 🔗 ⁠
⁠
Text by Lavanya Chopra @lavanyachopra⁠
Photography by Roshan Satish Paliath @roshan_paliath

For this city in Maharashtra, the historical bones of basalt stone and wadas continue to oscillate between the old and new — in Pune, a residence designed for a Maharashtrian family by INI Design Studio @amol.0910 reflects this exchange of cultural sensibilities. The two-bedroom space narrates its elements quietly; think handcrafted objects rooted in local traditions that meet clean lines and functional planning. 

Much like the large pillars that support the roofs of old-world homes, a hand-carved column stands like a watchful sentinel, while a green interlocking star-patterned floor leads the way into the living room. “The design draws inspiration from nature, heritage craftsmanship and slow living. We explored tactile richness, curated contrasts and subtle detailing to bring soul into every corner,” says Amol Nalavade. However, the home doesn’t part away from the idea of less is more, where materials are chosen such that age with grace. 

#EDHomes Tour the home from the link in our bio!

A pooja room thoughtfully designed to align with the living room’s overall design language. A simple yet traditional unit, enhanced with delicate brass bells and a beautifully crafted lotus handle, brings the entire look together with elegance and grace. The intentional use of fluted glass ensures a sense of privacy, softly concealing the interiors when the doors are closed while still allowing light to pass through.

• K A L P A V R I K S H A •

Master Bedroom 

First attempt at bringing traditional design colors to life-bold, vibrant, and daring to break the mold. Exploring fresh possibilities while honoring timeless root.

Every project has a story, but some resonate deeper. This home, designed for an Air Officer who has lived with his family across the length and breadth of India, carries with it the spirit of journeys, traditions, and belonging. What made the process truly special was the enthusiasm and involvement of the family-matching our passion at every stage of design and execution. From site work to craftsmanship, each step reflects not just a physical build but a weaving together of memories, cultures, and values into a timeless, traditional home. Architecture, at its finest, is never only about walls and spaces—it’s about creating a place for life’s stories to unfold with grace and warmth✨

Project : Kalpavriksha
Design by : @hastayu_  @laxmi_uppin

fullname: Volume Zero Architecture & Interior Design Magazine
description:
Dhwani by T Square Architects

In a time dominated by concrete skylines and glass façades, Dhwani stands apart as a refreshing architectural narrative one that harmoniously blends the wisdom of Kerala’s traditional design with the clarity of modern minimalism. Surrounded by lush greenery, it is more than a residence; it is a calm, introspective retreat that reconnects its inhabitants with both nature and their cultural roots.

At the heart of Dhwani lies an open-to-sky central courtyard, inspired by the essence of Kerala’s vernacular homes. Traditionally a source of light and ventilation, here it becomes the soul of the house an immersive space that invites gathering, quiet reflection, and everyday pause. By dissolving the boundaries between indoors and outdoors, the courtyard allows nature to actively shape the spatial experience within.

𝗣𝗥𝗢𝗝𝗘𝗖𝗧 𝗗𝗘𝗧𝗔𝗜𝗟𝗦
Project Name: Dhwani 
Location: Malappuram,Kerala,India
Built Area:  2990 sq. ft.
Approx Cost: Undisclosed
Completion Year: 202

A warm take on timeless design. Earthy tones, soft patterns, and layered materials create a bathroom that feels both classic and alive.
From the moment one steps inside, Padmalaya whispers a sense of arrival — a place where the lotus blooms not merely in name, but in the lived experience of its residents.
Layered greens, balanced textures. Matte surfaces, subtle patterns, and warm wood tones come together to create a timeless bathroom space.
